segments.s312-g33: An optative blessing: the compound جهان‌آفرین (“world‑creator”) is vocative/subject meaning God or the Creator; رهنمای is a noun ‘guide’ and تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun. باد here is the classical Persian optative particle ‘may’ (not the noun ‘wind’). Word order is elevated/poetic (noun phrase + noun + pronoun + optative).
segments.s312-g34: همیشه ‘always’ modifies the following clause; سر تخت is a compound meaning ‘top/summit of the throne’ (سر here = top), جای تو = ‘your place’ (تو = thou/you, poetic). باد again is the optative ‘may’. The phrase follows Persian poetic inversion (adverb → locative phrase → possessor → optative).
segments.s312-g35: نهادند is the past 3rd‑person plural ‘they placed/put’; بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’; نامه‌بر is ‘letter‑bearer/messenger’. مهر شاه is ‘the king’s seal’ (poetic omission of the ezāfe is common). Together: ‘they placed the king’s seal upon the messenger.’
segments.s312-g36: بر ایوان شه is a prepositional/locative phrase ‘to/on the king’s iwan/portico’ (شه = archaic form of شاه). گیو is the proper name Giv (subject), بگزید is past 3rd‑singular ‘chose/selected’ and راه is ‘way/path’. Word order places the locative phrase first for poetic emphasis: ‘toward the royal portico, Giv chose the way.’
segments.s312-g37: به ره بر نبودش is an idiomatic/past construction: نبودش = ‘there was not for him’ (negative past with enclitic -ش), meaning ‘he had no (time for) stay on the road’. به جایی درنگ = ‘a pause at any place’ (درنگ = pause/tarry). Persian here uses compact negative existential phrasing rather than a verbal auxiliary.
segments.s312-g38: به نزدیک = ‘near’/‘approaching’; کیخسرو is the proper name Kay Khosrow; آمد is past 3rd‑singular ‘came’. به گنگ is an adverbial phrase ‘in a mute/speechless manner’ (گنگ = mute). The clause describes approach plus manner: ‘he came up near Kay Khosrow, mute/speechless.’
segments.s312-g39: آفرین کرد is an idiom: آفرین (acclamation/praise) + کرد (do) = ‘he praised’ or ‘he extolled him’; بر او marks the target ‘upon him/for him’. و connects the next clause; نامه بداد in classical usage means ‘(he) gave the letter’—بداد here functions as a past giving verb in older/poetic usage (note: the provided lemma senses did not list ‘to give’, so that specific sense is archaic/poetic).
segments.s312-g40: پیام = ‘message’. نیا here is treated as the name/identifier of the messenger (a proper name or title), so the provided nominal senses do not match and are marked -1. پیش او = ‘before him / in his presence’ (prepositional phrase). کرد is the past ‘did/made’ auxiliary and یاد (‘remembrance/ reminder’) with کرد yields ‘caused to remember’ = ‘reminded’. The clause thus says the messenger Nia presented the message before him and reminded him.
segments.s312-g121: برفتند is a past plural verb (رفتند) with the prefix بر- giving a sense of moving toward/forward; in classical/poetic Persian the verb can appear before its subjects. فغفور و خاقان چین are the coordinated subjects (Faghfur = Byzantine emperor; خاقان چین = the Khagan of China), joined by و.
segments.s312-g122: بر here is a preposition meaning 'toward/to' followed by شاه as its object. با introduces an instrumental/comitative phrase 'with' and links the nouns پوزش and آفرین (joined by و) which enumerate the manner of approach; both nouns are given in their nominal senses.
segments.s312-g123: سه منزل is a numeral + noun phrase meaning 'three stations/stages' (منزل here = stage of a journey). ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from', چین names the origin, پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the king', and آمدند is the past plural 'they came'.
segments.s312-g124: خود is an emphatic pronoun 'himself/themselves' coordinating with نامداران 'the renowned/notable ones' by و; به راه آمدند is a light-verb construction where به + راه indicates movement 'onto/to the road' and آمدند is the past plural verb 'came'.
segments.s312-g125: همه راه آباد کرده is a perfect/participial construction: آباد کرده = 'having made prosperous' modifies the preceding object 'the whole road' (همه راه). چو introduces a simile 'as/like' and دست is the simile's image ('as if by a hand'). Word order (object + participle) and the omitted subject are typical of epic-poetic style.
segments.s312-g126: در و دشت is a paired noun phrase ('towns/gates and plains') joined by و. چون is another simile/linker 'as/like', جایگاه is 'place/dwelling', and نشست is the past verb 'sat/settled' (here meaning 'came to rest' / 'were settled').
segments.s312-g127: همه بوم و بر is an enumerative phrase 'the whole land and realm' (بوم و بر is a fixed pair meaning homeland/land). پوشش و خوردنی lists goods 'coverings (clothing/coverings) and food' — nouns coordinated with و. The noun list is presented as things provided to the region.
segments.s312-g128: از is the preposition 'from/of', آرایش here means 'adornment/array' and governs بزم 'banquet/feast' (آرایش بزم = the array/arrangement of the feast). و links گستردنی, an adjective/participle meaning 'that which is spread/for spreading'; the whole phrase indicates source: 'from the feast's array and the items to be spread.'
segments.s312-g177: فرستاده را گفت: a compact reported-speech construction where گفت (said/told) introduces an imperative; فرستاده + را marks the addressee ('to the messenger'). برگرد is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'return/turn back.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' رو is an imperative/verb meaning 'turn (your face) / go toward' and links directly to the following prepositional phrase.
segments.s312-g178: به نزدیک آن بدگمان باز شو: به + نزدیک forms a prepositional phrase 'toward / close to'; آن بدگمان = 'that (one) who is suspicious' (آن + adjective). باز شو is an imperative made of باز ('open' / 'again') + شو (imperative of شدن 'become'), here functioning as 'show yourself / appear / be open again.'
segments.s312-g179: بگویش is the imperative 'tell him' (بگو + enclitic -یش = 'to him'); که introduces a content clause 'that.' از گردش تیره‌روز literally 'from the turning/cycle of ill-days' = 'after the change of your ill fortune'; گردش here means 'turn/turning (change of fortune)' and تیره‌روز is an adjective-noun meaning 'ill-fated / dark-day / unlucky.'
segments.s312-g180: تو گشتی چنین شاد و گیتی فروز: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you.' گشتی is a past/perfective form 'you have become / you turned into' used here to state the result. چنین = 'thus/so.' شاد = 'happy.' و links the coordinated predicate. گیتی فروز is a compound-like pairing 'world' + 'that which lights/brightens' (فروز used adjectivally/participially), i.e. 'world-illuminating' or 'glorious in the world.'
segments.s312-g181: ببینی is a present/subjunctive form used here with future sense 'you will see.' چو = 'when.' آیی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of آمدن 'you come.' ز ما is the poetic genitive/prepositional 'upon/from us' meaning 'toward us / against us.' دستبرد is a noun meaning an assault/encroachment/raid—'when you come to attack/raid us.'
segments.s312-g182: بدانی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of دانستن used here with future force 'you will know.' که introduces the clause content. مردان کدامند = 'which men they are' (کدامند = 'which are they'). و گرد ties on another element: گرد here names the band/host/round (a poetic term for a troop or company), so the phrase says you'll know both who the men are and what kind of force they are.
segments.s312-g89: به درویش بخشیم بسیار چیز is a hortatory clause: به + indirect object (درویش) marks the beneficiary ('to/for the dervish'), followed by the verb بخشیم (first-person-plural jussive/subjunctive, 'let us give' / 'we should give'). بسیار چیز is the direct object 'many things' placed after the verb; word order (prepositional indirect object — verb — object) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s312-g90: نثار و خورشها بسازیم نیز continues the hortatory series: نثار (a noun 'offerings') coordinated with خورشها ('stews', plural) are the objects, and بسازیم is the same first-person-plural jussive 'let us make/prepare.' نیز at the end adds 'also/likewise.' Note the verb finally governs both coordinated nouns.
segments.s312-g91: فرستاده را بی‌کران هدیه داد is a simple past clause: فرستاده marked by را as the direct object 'the messenger,' بی‌کران ('boundless') qualifies هدیه ('gift'), and داد is the third-person-singular past 'gave.' Adjective precedes the noun it modifies (بی‌کران هدیه).
segments.s312-g92: بیامد به درگاه پیروز و شاد is another past clause: بیامد 'he came' (simple past), به درگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the court/presence,' and پیروز و شاد are adjectival descriptors ('victorious and joyful') applied to the subject (understood from the previous clause). The phrase can be read either as 'he came to the court, triumphant and glad' or less likely 'to the victorious and glad court.'
segments.s312-g93: دگر نامور چون به مکران رسید is a temporal clause: دگر نامور 'another renowned one' is the subject, چون functions as the conjunction 'as/when,' and رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached.' The phrase places the temporal conjunction before the verb in classical style.
segments.s312-g94: دل شاه مکران دگرگونه دید: دل شاه مکران = 'the heart of the king of Makran' (possessive sequence), دگرگونه 'different' (predicative) and دید 'saw' (past). Idiomatically this means the king's heart/feeling was altered or he felt differently; syntactically, دل is the grammatical subject/experiencer and دید is the past perception verb.
segments.s312-g95: بر تخت او رفت و نامه بداد contains two coordinated actions in the past: بر تخت او رفت 'he went upon his throne' (بر + تخت + pronoun + past verb رفت). In نامه بداد the object نامه 'the letter' is followed by بداد, here functioning as the past verb 'gave' (a classical/poetic variant of داد with an initial b-), so the clause means 'and he gave the letter.'
segments.s312-g96: بگفت از پیام آنچه بودش بیاد is a report clause: بگفت 'he said/recounted,' از پیام 'about the message' (about/concerning), آنچه 'that which/whatever,' بودش is the past copula with enclitic pronoun 'it was for him' (here idiomatically 'he had/remembered'), and بیاد literally 'in memory' — together yielding 'he recounted from the message whatever was in his memory' (i.e., whatever he remembered).
segments.s312-g57: 'همی گفت' is a literary past tense expression: 'همی' is a poetic/progressive particle (keeps the action vivid) and 'گفت' is the past verb 'he said'. 'کز' introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'from which'), and 'داور یک خدای' is an appositive description of God — 'داور' here is an epithet 'the Judge' applied to 'یک خدای' ('one God'). The whole piece links to the following verb 'بخواهم' (the content of what he said).
segments.s312-g58: 'بخواهم' is 1st person present-subjunctive (in reported speech) meaning 'I will / I would ask' used to report his intention after 'گفت'. 'که' introduces the subordinate clause of result/desired state. 'باشد' is 3rd person present subjunctive/optative 'may be'/'be (so that)'. The clitic 'م' + the object marker 'را' together form 'مرا' (object 'me') split in the text; 'را' is the definite-object marker. 'رهنمای' is the direct object 'guide' (i.e., 'that he be my guide').
segments.s312-g59: 'مگر' here is a rhetorical/conditional particle meaning 'unless' / 'or else' (introducing an alternative). 'همچنین' modifies the following action as 'in the same way' / 'likewise'. 'خون افراسیاب' is a genitive phrase 'the blood of Afrasiab' (خون = blood, افراسیاب = proper name).
segments.s312-g60: 'هم' is an adverb here ('also' / 'too' — emphasis). 'ایدر' is an archaic locative 'here'. 'بریزم' is 1st-person present-subjunctive / optative 'I should pour' or 'let me pour' (the b- prefix + present stem is used in wishes/conditional). 'به کردار' is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of' ('به' + 'کردار' = 'like / as'). 'آب' is literal 'water' — 'به کردار آب' = 'as if water' or 'like water'.
segments.s312-g61: 'و' is the coordinating 'and'. 'ز' (poetic for از) means 'from'. 'آن جایگه' = 'that place' (جایگه = جایگاه, place/position). 'شد' is the past verb meaning here 'moved / went' (in the idiom 'شد سوی' = 'turned toward'). 'سوی تخت' = 'toward the throne' ('سوی' = toward, 'تخت' = throne). 'باز' placed after the phrase means 'again' / 'returned'.
segments.s312-g62: 'همی گفت' again is the literary 'he said' (همی keeps the narration vivid). 'با' is the preposition 'with'. 'داور پاک' = 'the pure Judge' (داور here again an epithet of God; پاک = pure, adjective after noun is normal in poetry). 'راز' is the object 'secret' — 'he spoke a secret with (or to) the pure Judge' i.e., he confided/held a private conversation. Word order is poetic but straightforward: verb + prepositional phrase + object.
segments.s312-g63: 'ز' = 'from'. 'لشکر فرستادگان' is a genitive construction: 'envoys of the army' = 'envoys from the army'. 'برگزید' is the past tense verb 'he chose / selected'. The clause thus states he selected envoys out of the army.
segments.s312-g64: 'که' introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'). 'گویند' and 'دانند' are present-subjunctive forms meaning 'that they may speak / negotiate' and 'that they may know / find out' (purpose of the envoys). The pair 'گفت و شنید' immediately after are abstract nouns or verbal nouns ('speaking and hearing') used to summarize the envoys' business — 'to speak and to hear' or 'speech and listening'. Grammatically, the clause strings subjunctive verbs (purpose) and then nominalized actions as an emphatic summary.
segments.s312-g81: که here is the subordinating 'that' introducing the reported clause. Word order is Subject (ما) + (standard marked with را) شاه را + adverbial intensifier سر به سر + predicate adjective کهتریم ('we are inferior'); سر به سر is an idiomatic intensifier meaning 'completely/utterly', and کهتریم is first‑person plural present (archaic/poetic) of the adjective 'کهتر' (‘inferior’).
segments.s312-g82: زمین is the noun 'the land/country'. جز ... indicates exception: 'except by his command' (جز + به فرمان او). نسپریم is the negative present/subjunctive form (ن‑ prefix) of سپردن, here meaning 'we do not/shall not entrust/surrender'; به فرمان او uses به = 'by/at' to mark 'at his command'.
segments.s312-g83: گذرها is the plural 'passes/passages'. که introduces a relative clause 'through/which'. راه دلیران is 'the road/path of the brave (warriors)'; older Persian omits an explicit ezāfe. بدست here functions as a postpositional/ adverbial 'by/through' linking the relative clause to the verb that follows (i.e. 'the passes through which the warriors' road runs').
segments.s312-g84: ببینیم is first‑person plural subjunctive/optative 'let us see' or 'we should see' (used to express purpose/intention). تا چند is an idiomatic 'how long' (تا carrying the sense 'until/for how long' and چند 'how much/how long'). ویران شدست is an archaic/perfect construction 'has become ruined' (شدست = 'has become/it became'), describing the state of being desolate.
segments.s312-g85: کنیم is first‑person plural optative/subjunctive 'let us do/make'. از سر آباد is an idiom 'from the head/from the start, making (it) prosperous again' (سر used idiomatically 'again/afresh' + آباد 'prosperous'). با خوردنی literally 'with edibles/provisions' modifies the action: they will restore prosperity by supplying food.
segments.s312-g86: بباشیم is subjunctive/optative first‑person plural 'let us be' or 'may we be' (here: let us be in that state / generous). و is the coordinating 'and'. آریمش carries an enclitic -ش 'it' (object) on a form related to آرَییدن/'to arrange, to make settled' (here used poetically; glosses given include 'tranquility/repose'), and آوردنی is a deverbal adjective/gerund 'to be brought' or 'for bringing' — together implying preparing/arranging things to be brought.
segments.s312-g87: همی گفت is a classical/perfective-like iterative past: 'he kept saying' or 'he said repeatedly/continually'. هر کس که بودش خرد is a relative/indefinite clause 'whoever had wisdom' — بودش is past 'was' with enclitic (he had/it was to him) and خرد means 'wisdom/sense'.
segments.s312-g88: که‌گر is a conditional particle 'if/should (someone)' or 'whoever; if'. بی‌زیان 'without harm/harmless' qualifies او 'he'. به ما بگذرد uses به = 'to/towards' and بگذرد (subjunctive/indicative) 'he passes/comes by/comes to us' — together 'if he comes to us unharmed'.
segments.s312-g73: بیاراست (from آراستن) here means 'make ready/array' and is used with باید to express necessity; the poet places the verb before باید (inverted word order) for rhythm, but the sense is 'it is necessary to make the army ready for battle.' را marks سپه as the definite/seen object and به رزم is an adverbial phrase 'for/in battle.'
segments.s312-g74: هر آن کس که is a relative/indefinite head phrase meaning 'whoever/anyone who.' بگریزد is third‑person singular present/subjunctive (classical usage) 'flees/should flee' used here as a simple relative 'who flees.' از راه بزم = 'from the way/path of the bāzm,' where بزم means 'banquet/assembly.'
segments.s312-g75: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause 'the messenger came.' به هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase 'to every country/realm' (کشوری = 'of the realm' here used adjectivally).
segments.s312-g76: به هر جا که = 'to every place that/where.' بد نامور is a compound adjective meaning 'ill‑famed/poorly‑renowned' (بد functions adverbially/adjectivally to invert the sense of نامور). مهتری here refers to the chief/preeminent person; the phrase describes places where the chief was of bad repute.
segments.s312-g77: غمی گشت is a nominal‑verb combination 'sorrow/grief became (fell upon).' فغفور (title) and خاقان چین are named rulers ('the Byzantine emperor' and 'the Khagan of China'); و simply links the two titles.
segments.s312-g78: بزرگان = 'the great ones' i.e. nobles/leading men. هر کشوری = 'of every realm' and همچنین = 'likewise/similarly,' so the clause says these nobles were affected in the same way.
segments.s312-g79: فرستاده را marks 'the messenger' as the definite object/indirect object 'to the messenger.' چند گفتند literally 'they said a few/several (things)' (چند = 'several') and گفتند is the past 3rd‑plural 'they said/spoke.' گرم modifies the manner of their speech: 'warmly/with zeal.'
segments.s312-g80: سخنهای (plural) = 'words/speeches' and شیرین qualifies them as 'sweet/delightful' (pleasant words). به آواز نرم is an adverbial phrase of manner 'in a soft/gentle voice' (به + noun = 'with/by [a voice]' here expressing how the words were spoken).
segments.s312-g129: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک modifies the following noun and means 'drawing near/approaching'. اندر آمد is a verb phrase 'came in/arrived at' with the subject سپاه placed after the verb (poetic inversion); literal order is 'when the army came near the king.'
segments.s312-g130: ببستند is past plural 'they tied/fastened' (here 'they strung/put up'). آذین is the direct object 'decorations/festoons'. به marks the location/target ('on/along'), and بیراه و راه are the places modified ('byways and the road'); word order places verb before its object, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s312-g131: به + noun marks the location/goal ('upon/onto the walls'). دیبا (brocade) is the cloth hung, and برآویختند is past plural meaning 'they hung up' (literally 'they suspended upon'). The verb comes at the end in typical poetic order.
segments.s312-g132: ز بر here sets up a surface/positional relation ('on/upon' in poetic usage — literally 'from upon'), followed by the items scattered: زعفران (saffron) and درم (dirhams). ریختند is past plural 'they poured/they scattered' and sits at the end of the clause.
segments.s312-g133: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. با + شاه فغفور means 'toward/with the emperor (the Byzantine emperor)'. گستاخ is an adjectival predicate 'insolent/audacious', and شد is the copular/change-of-state verb 'became' (past).
segments.s312-g134: به پیش اندر آمد = 'came forward/into the presence' (به + پیش marks movement toward the front/presence; اندر آمد = 'entered/arrived in'). سوی کاخ شد = 'he turned/moved toward the palace' where سوی marks direction and شد again expresses the resulting movement/state; verbs appear at the clause end in poetic order.
segments.s312-g135: بدو is an enclitic/phrase meaning 'to him' ('said to him'). گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (introducing reported speech). ما is the subject 'we', شاه را uses را as an object/topic marker emphasizing 'the king', and کهتریم is a first-person-plural predicative/adjective 'we are lesser/inferior'.
segments.s312-g136: اگر begins a conditional 'if'. کهتری here refers to 'the lesser (portion/status)'. را is again the object/topic marker; خود 'ourselves/ourselves proper' emphasizes the agent; اندر is the locative/prepositional particle 'in/into', and خوریم is first-person-plural present/subjunctive of خوردن 'to eat' ('we eat/should eat'). The clause is conditional and elliptical in tone (typical of poetic speech).
segments.s312-g97: Literally 'سبک مر فرستاده را خوار کرد' = 'treated my messenger lightly / humiliated my messenger.' سبك here is used adverbially ('lightly, with scant regard'); مر is an archaic enclitic meaning 'my' (possession) attached to the preceding word; فرستاده را is the definite direct object (را marks the object); خوار کرد is a compound verb meaning 'made low / humiliated' (کرد is the light verb 'to make/do'). Word order is Subject/Agent implied → manner (سبک) → possessed object (مر فرستاده را) → predicate (خوار کرد).
segments.s312-g98: دل انجمن پر ز تیمار کرد literally 'made the heart of the assembly full of care.' دل انجمن is a genitive-like construction ('the heart of the council/assembly') without an overt ezāfe (poetic compression); پر ز + noun means 'full of X' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = 'of/from'); کرد is the causative/light verb 'made/caused' — so the clause means 'he caused the assembly's heart to be filled with worry/care.'
segments.s312-g99: بدو گفت با شاه ایران بگوی = 'He said to him: tell the Shah of Iran...' بدو is the clitic/prepositional phrase به + او = 'to him'; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech or an order); با before شاه functions as the prepositional marker introducing the addressee/target ('to/toward the Shah of Iran') and بگوی is a jussive/imperative form of گفتن meaning 'say / tell' (used here as a directive: 'tell [the Shah]'). Word order: indirect object (بدو) → speech verb (گفت) → imperative clause (با شاه ایران بگوی).
segments.s312-g100: که نادیده بر ما فزونی مجوی is the content of the order introduced by که ('that'). نادیده here functions adverbially ('unseen' / 'in ignorance' / 'without knowledge'); بر ما = 'over us' (بر used figuratively = 'above/against us'); فزونی = 'superiority/excess' and مجوی is the negative jussive/subjunctive 'do not seek' (archaic/poetic negative imperative). Together: 'that he should not, in ignorance, seek to be superior to us.'
segments.s312-g101: زمانه همه زیر تخت منست = 'All the world / all fortune is under my throne.' زمانه (here) = 'the world, the state of affairs' or 'fortune' used as the subject; همه 'all'; زیر تخت = 'under the throne' where زیر is used figuratively = 'subject to / under the control of'; منست is an archaic fused form ('من است' = 'is mine' / 'belongs to me' or 'is [under] me'). The clause asserts dominion: everything is under his throne.
segments.s312-g102: جهان روشن از فر بخت منست = 'The world is bright because of the glory of my fortune.' جهان 'the world' (subject); روشن 'bright' (predicate adjective); از here means 'because of' (indicating cause); فر بخت = 'the splendour/glory (فر) of fate/fortune (بخت)'; منست again = 'is mine / pertains to me' (archaicized copula). The phrase attributes the world's brightness to his royal glory and fate.
segments.s312-g103: چو خورشید تابان شود بر سپهر = 'When the shining sun becomes/appears upon the sky.' چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; خورشید تابان = noun + adjective ('the shining sun'); شود is the present/future/optative of شدن 'becomes / appears'; بر سپهر = locative/poetic 'upon the heavens/sky.' The clause gives a temporal setting ('when the sun rises/appears').
segments.s312-g104: نخستین بر این بوم تابد به مهر = 'it first shines on this land with favour.' نخستین here functions adverbially/ordinally 'first' or 'first of all'; بر این بوم = 'upon this land' (بوم = homeland/soil); تابد is the present tense of تافتن 'to shine' (poetic present); به مهر = 'with love/favour/kindness' (مهر used in the sense 'favour' or 'kindly regard'). Word order places the adverb (نخستین) before the locative and verb for emphasis.
segments.s312-g153: بیامد is third-person simple past ('he came'). چو here is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. نزدیک modifies the following place name, so نزدیک مکران رسید = 'reached near Makran' with رسید (third-person past) marking arrival; the clause literally reads 'he came, when (he) reached near Makran' with the subject omitted and understood from the verbs.
segments.s312-g154: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. لشکر is the host/army, جهاندیده‌ای ('جهان‌دیده' + the indefinite/partitive -ی) describes experienced or seasoned men, and برگزید is the third-person past 'he selected/chose'. The clause is accusative: 'he selected from the host some experienced men' with the subject again omitted and understood from the prior verbs.
segments.s312-g155: بر here functions as the directional preposition 'to/toward' (بر شاه مکران = 'to the king of Makran'). فرستاد is third-person past 'he sent' (implying a messenger or letter), and گفت is the past 'he said' introducing reported speech that follows.
segments.s312-g156: که introduces the reported clause ('that' / here functioning like 'may it be that...'). با + شهریاران is a prepositional phrase 'with the lords/princes'; خرد is used adjectivally ('wise'/'of wisdom') modifying شهریاران; باد is the optative particle ('may' — an archaic/poetic wish marker), and جفت is 'pair/companion' used predicatively: 'may you be paired/matched with wise lords.'
segments.s312-g157: خورش here means 'food/provisions' (literally a stew or cooked food). ساز functions as an imperative 'make/prepare' (an older/poetic command form related to 'ساختن'/'آراستن'), followed by راه سپاه (the route/march of the army) and مرا marking possession ('my') attached to سپاه; the whole phrase is an imperative: 'prepare food for my army's march.' Note older Persian allows this word order without izāfa.
segments.s312-g158: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well'/'in a good manner'. بیارای is another imperative (from آراستن, 'adorn/arrange/prepare') meaning 'set in order' or 'make beautiful/fit', and گاه مرا (گاه = 'place/quarters' here; مرا = 'my') with را as the object marker yields 'make my quarters well-appointed.' The verb governs the following noun phrase as its definite object.
segments.s312-g159: نگه کن is an imperative idiom 'look' / 'take heed' ('نگه' noun 'look/watch' + کن imperative 'do'), and که introduces the subordinate content clause. ما از کجا رفته‌ایم is an embedded wh‑clause 'from where we have gone/come' with رفته‌ایم a compound past (رفته + ایم) meaning 'we have gone' or 'we came from.'
segments.s312-g160: This is a correlative negation using نه ... و ... و نه ... = 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'. مستیم is 'we are drunk/intoxicated', بیراه here means 'astray/errant' (not on the right road), and خفته‌ایم is 'we are asleep'/'we have slept' (first‑person plural perfective form). Together: 'we are neither drunk nor astray nor asleep.'
segments.s312-g65: فرستاد is a past-tense verb (he sent/dispatched). کس is an indefinite noun 'someone' as the direct object. نزد is a preposition here meaning 'in the presence of / to' and introduces the destination (خاقان چین = 'the Khaghan of China'). Word order is verb + object + prepositional destination, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s312-g66: به marks the destination 'to/unto' and links to two parallel titles: فغفور (a title, here the Byzantine emperor) and سالار (a title 'lord' or ruler) which governs مکران زمین — 'the land of Makran' (مکران = region, زمین = land/country). The coordinating و joins the two titles.
segments.s312-g67: که‌گر is a conditional particle 'if'. داد here means 'justice' (not the verb 'give'); گیرید is a present/subjunctive (or hortative/imperative) form meaning 'you take/maintain' — together داد گیرید = 'administer/maintain justice'. و links to فرمان کنید, where فرمان is 'order/command' and کنید is the 2pl subjunctive/imperative 'do/issue'. The clause is a conditional instruction.
segments.s312-g68: ز is the causal 'because of / from'. کردار بد is a noun phrase 'evil deed(s) / bad conduct'. دل پشیمان کنید is a causative construction: دل (heart) + پشیمان (regretful) + کنید (make/bring about) — 'make hearts regret' or 'cause hearts to repent'. The final کنید is causative/subjunctive 'cause/make'.
segments.s312-g69: خورشها is the plural noun 'stews/provisions' (the -ها plural suffix). فرستید is a past or imperative 2pl/3pl form meaning 'send' (here an order). نزد + سپاه marks the destination 'to the army / before the army'. The phrase is an instruction to dispatch supplies to the troops.
segments.s312-g70: ببینید is a 2nd-person plural imperative/subjunctive 'see/ensure' (literally 'see! / make sure'). ناچار is an adjective/adverb 'compelled/forced' modifying the following object phrase ما را 'us' (را is the definite object marker). به راه is a directional phrase 'onto the road / into the way'. The clause therefore commands: 'see that they force us onto the road.'
segments.s312-g71: کسی is an indefinite 'someone / anyone'. کو is an archaic/poetic relative/interrogative particle here functioning like 'who' or 'that' (classical Persian often uses کو where modern Persian uses که/که او). بتابد is a present/subjunctive/optative of تافتن meaning 'to turn/bend' — figuratively 'to turn away' or 'deviate'. ز + فرمان من = 'from my command'. The whole segment says 'anyone who turns away from my command.'
segments.s312-g72: و‌گر is a conditional 'and if / but if'. دور باشد is a subordinate clause: دور 'distant/removed' and باشد is the subjunctive/present 'be' (here giving a conditional sense: 'if he be distant'). ز پیمان من = 'from my covenant/treaty'. Together: 'and if he be distant from my covenant.'
segments.s312-g169: همه شهر مکران = a noun phrase meaning “the whole/entire city of Makran”; تو is the 2nd-person singular pronoun (subject) placed before the verb; ویران کنی is a 2nd-person singular verbal construction (کنی, present/subjunctive form used in classical Persian to express what you will do or would do) meaning “you make/lay waste.” Word order is object/NP — subject — verb, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s312-g170: چو introduces a subordinate temporal/conditional clause (“when/if”); بر is the preposition marking direction/tendency toward something; کینه is the goal or content (‘hatred’); آهنگ here means ‘intention/aim’ (the aim set toward hatred); شیران is the group whose intention is being set (literally “lions,” used for brave warriors); کنی is again 2nd-person singular ‘you make/bring about.’ The clause literally says ‘when you make the lions’ aim toward hatred.’
segments.s312-g171: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause ‘the messenger came.’ پیامش is پیام + enclitic ش ‘his message’ (direct object). بداد here functions as an older/poetic past verbal form meaning ‘gave’ or ‘delivered’ (ب‑ prefixed to a past stem is common in classical Persian), so پیامش بداد = ‘he delivered/gave his message.’
segments.s312-g172: نبد is a negative existential (classical form of نبود) meaning ‘there was not’ or ‘there lacked’; بر دلش = ‘upon/in his heart’ (بر used as ‘on’); جای = ‘place’ (there was no place); پیغام و داد = ‘message and justice’ (two nouns joined by و). The whole clause is an existential negative: ‘there was no place in his heart for message or for justice.’
segments.s312-g173: سر بی‌خرد = ‘the head (of) the foolish (man)’ (noun + postpositive adjective); زان = archaic contracted form meaning ‘from that’ or ‘because of that’; سخن = ‘speech’ or ‘words’; خیره شد = past of خیره شدن ‘to be stunned/struck dumb/to stare’ (شد = became). The clause: ‘the foolish man’s head became stunned at that speech.’
segments.s312-g174: بجوشید is the past/inchoative form (ب‑ + جوشید) meaning ‘it boiled’ or ‘it seethed’ — here figurative for inner agitation; و = ‘and’; مغزش = مغز + ش ‘his brain’; از آن = ‘from that’ / ‘because of that’ (causal); تیره شد = ‘became dark/clouded’ (شد = became). Together: ‘it seethed, and his brain was clouded/darkened by that.’
segments.s312-g175: پراگنده لشکر = ‘the scattered/widely spread host’ (adjective + noun); همه = ‘all/the whole’ modifying the host; گرد کرد is a compound where گرد ‘around’ + کرد ‘made/did’ = ‘gathered’ (گرد کردن = to gather). The clause means ‘he gathered the whole scattered army together.’
segments.s312-g176: بیاراست is a past/poetic verbal form meaning ‘he arrayed/ordered/arranged’ (بیاراست = he set in order/adorned for battle); بر دشت = ‘on the plain’ (prepositional phrase); جای نبرد = ‘the place of battle’ = the battlefield or positions for fighting. Together: ‘he drew them up on the plain in the place/array for battle.’
segments.s312-g113: نمانم is a first-person present negative form (literally “I do not remain / I will not let it remain”) introducing a subordinate clause with که. The clause بر بومِ من بگذری places the preposition بر + noun بوم (my land/soil) before the verb; بگذری is a 2nd-person singular present-subjunctive/optative ('you pass' / 'you should pass') and sits at the clause end, as is usual in classical Persian.
segments.s312-g114: This clause continues the idea introduced by نمانم که. وز here is an archaic/poetic prepositional form (behaving as از / 'from'); این مرز جایی is a noun phrase 'this frontier/place', and به پی بسپری is a verb phrase where به پی ('in/for pursuit' or 'to follow') precedes بسپری (2nd-person singular present-subjunctive of سپردن 'to entrust/send'). The verbs are parallel: both predicates (بگذری, بسپری) are subordinate to the initial نمانم که.
segments.s312-g115: Again نمانم begins the sentence ('I will not allow / it shall not remain that'). مانی is a proper name in vocative/subject position, followed by the emphatic pronoun تو; پیروزگر is a predicative noun/adjective 'victorious' placed after the subject, following the typical classical Persian order Subject — (pronoun) — Predicator.
segments.s312-g116: وگر is a conditional conjunction 'but if'. یابی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of یافتن/یابیدن meaning 'you obtain/you find' (here 'you gain'). از اختر نیک is a prepositional phrase 'from/through a good/auspicious star' (a poetic way to say 'by good fortune'), and the final بر is a small particle/preposition used poetically (linking the phrase to what follows or giving an adverbial sense). The clause is conditional and elliptical, typical of epic diction.
segments.s312-g117: بر این‌گونه is a prepositional/temporal phrase 'upon this sort of [thing] / at such a reply'. چون here introduces the temporal clause ('when'), شاه is the subject, پاسخ the object 'answer', and شنید is the past-tense verb 'he heard', placed at the end in normal SOV order.
segments.s312-g118: از آن جایگه is a prepositional phrase 'from that place/position', لشکر is the subject 'the army', اندر is an adverb/preposition 'in / within / from', and کشید is the past 3rd-person singular of کشیدن here meaning 'drew (back) / moved' or 'withdrew' (a martial usage). Word order places the verb at the end.
segments.s312-g119: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (or 'arrived/advanced'). گرازان is a plural noun 'boars' (or metaphorically 'troop of boar-like fighters') as the subject. به سوی + place is the directional phrase 'toward X' and here ختن is the proper name Khotan (a place); the prepositional phrase follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s312-g120: جهاندار is a title 'world-ruler / sovereign' (here the leader), با is the preposition 'with', نامدار is an adjective 'famed/renowned', and انجمن is 'assembly/retinue'. The phrase means 'the sovereign, with his renowned retinue' and is an appositive/continuation describing the arriving force.
segments.s312-g9: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by' placed before دست تو ('your hand'), where دست is the noun and تو is the enclitic pronoun/agent. آواره is a predicate adjective 'displaced/wandering' and شد is the past copula 'became'; در جهان is a locative adverbial placed after the verb phrase ('in the world').
segments.s312-g10: نگویند is a negative present/gnomic form meaning 'they do not say/mention' (n- + گویند). نامش is name + enclitic 'his' (ش) and functions as the direct object. جز اندر نهان is an exception/adverbial phrase 'except in secret' (جز = 'except', اندر = 'in/within', نهان = 'secretly/hidden').
segments.s312-g11: همه‌ساله is an adverb 'yearly/year after year' placed up front. تا بود is a temporal clause 'as long as he was/while he lived' (تا + بود), and خونریز بود is the main predication 'he was blood‑shedding' with بود as the past copula.
segments.s312-g12: به here marks the state or condition ('in/with'), introducing بدنامی و زشتی ('infamy and ugliness'); آویز is used predicatively ('hanging/attached to') and بود is the copula 'was'. The whole means he was figuratively 'hung on' infamy and disgrace.
segments.s312-g13: بزد is the past verb 'he struck/struck down' (verb before object). گردن is the direct object 'neck', followed by the proper name نوذر in apposition, with تاجدار as an epithet after the name ('the crowned').
segments.s312-g14: ز is the preposition 'from/of' introducing a genitive phrase; شاهان = 'the kings'. وز here is an archaic/coordinating element joining phrases (the precise gloss is contextually 'and/of'), followed by راستان 'the righteous'; یادگار is a noun 'memorial/memento' and completes the appositive idea 'a memorial of kings and of the righteous.'
segments.s312-g15: This segment lists epithets joined by و. برادرکش, بدتن, and شاه‌کش are compound/descriptive nouns/adjectives: 'brother‑killer/fratricide', 'ill‑bodied/ill‑favored', and 'شاه + کش' where کش is an agentive element forming 'king‑killer'.
segments.s312-g16: Another run of epithets joined by و: بداندیش 'malicious/ill‑minded' and بدراه 'on a wrong/bad path'. آشفته is an adjectival predicate 'disturbed/confused', and هش is an archaic exclamatory particle ('Alas!'/'Woe!') closing the line.
segments.s312-g41: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/from' placed before its noun phrase; گفتار او = 'his speech/words' (noun + pronoun). شاد شد is a compound verb (شاد + شد) meaning 'became glad' with شد as a past/resultative 'become'. The subject شهریار comes after the predicate (inverted order common in epic poetry): 'the king became glad.'
segments.s312-g42: می‌آورد is the 3rd-person imperfective/habitual form used in narrative to tell past action ('he brought' / 'he would bring'). The following nouns رامشگر and میگسار are objects/appositive complements introduced after the verb and joined with و: 'he brought a comforter and a cupbearer.'
segments.s312-g43: همی is a continuous/habitual particle (emphasizing ongoing action). خورد here is used as a narrative verb meaning 'spent/experienced' (a usage related to 'befall/happen'), so همی خورد پیروز و شادان سه روز = 'he spent/kept (the time) victorious and joyful for three days.'
segments.s312-g44: چو here means 'when.' بفروخت is a past form of فروختن ('sold' / 'gave up'); گیتی فروز is a compound/epithet literally 'world-brightener' (an epithet or descriptive phrase). The clause therefore reads 'when (the) world-illuminator gave/sold (it) / gave it up'—a short temporal subordinate clause following the three days.
segments.s312-g45: سپه را uses را as the definite/object marker ('to the army' with topical emphasis). همه modifies the following nouns. ترک و جوشن are coordinated direct objects meaning 'Turks and armor.' The final word بداد in the printed senses list does not match the verbal sense 'he gave/handed' needed here, so that specific lexical sense is not available in the provided list.
segments.s312-g46: پیام نیا is a noun phrase (likely 'the message of Nia' or a name). پیششان is a compound prepositional/adverbial phrase 'before them / in their presence' (پیش + شان). کرد is the simple past of کردن 'made/did' and combines with یاد 'remembrance/message' to mean 'he made [it] remembered / he made them remember' or 'he presented the message to them.'
segments.s312-g47: مر آن را = 'that (thing) to me' or 'that to him/me' depending on earlier context; مر functions as a datival pronoun. به + اسم introduces the recipient: 'to گستهم نوذر' (proper names: Gostaham and Nowzar). سپرد is the past tense 'entrusted/handed over' (سپردن). The phrase thus means 'he entrusted that to Gostaham Nowzar.' Note: گستهم in the provided senses is parsed as a verb form ('I wandered'), but here it functions as a name (hence not matched in the provided sense list).
segments.s312-g48: یکی is an indefinite/article-like word 'a certain' or 'one.' لشکری is a substantive adjective meaning 'military / a troop' (here noun: 'a troop of soldiers'). نامبردار is an adjective 'renowned/celebrated.' گرد in epic usage often names a 'band/force' but that noun-sense is not present in the provided senses, so it is left unassigned here.
segments.s312-g105: همم is a poetic noun 'my aspiration / my aim' and is the sentence topic; دانش و گنج آباد is the nominal predicate that follows the copula. هست is the copula 'is' (here merged as the final predicate). Word order is topic (همم) + predicate (دانش و گنج آباد) + copula (هست).
segments.s312-g106: This coordinated noun phrase (بزرگی و مردی و نیروی دست) continues the predicate begun in the previous segment and has no independent verb; the three nouns are joined by و. نیروی is a genitive/attributive 'strength (of)', linking to دست 'hand'.
segments.s312-g107: گر introduces a conditional ('if'). از من is the source/indirect object 'from me'. همی is an archaic/emphatic/progressive particle used with present verbs. راه جوید is the verb phrase 'seeks a way' (جوید is present), and روا + ست (رواست) is a predicative adjective plus copula meaning 'it is fitting/permissible.'
segments.s312-g108: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for/that'). هر جانور is the generic subject 'every creature/animal'; بر زمین is a locative phrase 'on the earth/ground'; پادشاست is the predicate, an older fused form meaning 'is king/sovereign' (پادشاه است).
segments.s312-g109: نبندیم is the 1st‑person plural negative of بستن ('we will not / we do not close/block'). اگر بگذری is the conditional clause 'if you pass' (بگذری is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive). بر تو راه is a prepositional/dative phrasing meaning 'the way for you' (بر + تو + راه), completing the sense 'we will not bar the way for you if you pass.'
segments.s312-g110: زیانی مکن is an imperative negative: زیانی (noun 'harm/loss') is the object and مکن is 'do not do.' بر گذر is a locative 'on/upon the pass' and با سپاه specifies instrument/means 'with an army.' Together: 'do not harm the pass with an army.'
segments.s312-g111: ور is a conditional/concessive 'if' (poetic). ایدون here is an adverbial/poetic particle (literally related to 'coming') used to mark time/occasion ('should/ever'), and که introduces the subordinate clause. با لشکر آیی is the verb phrase 'you come with an army' (آیی is 2nd‑person present), and به شهر is the destination 'to the city.'
segments.s312-g112: بر این پادشاهی is the prepositional phrase 'over/for this kingship.' ترا is the clitic combination 'for you' (ت = 2nd‑person, را = object/ topical marker here fused). نیست is the negated existential 'there is not / is not,' and بهر is used as 'share/advantage/claim' — so 'there is no claim/benefit for you in this kingship.'
segments.s312-g49: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گنگ here names a place/river (the Ganges) or else appears as an unusual/archaic proper-name usage; گزین is a verb/adjective 'chosen/choosing' or part of a verbal phrase meaning 'chose'; راه چین is the object 'the road to China' (راه = road, چین = China). برگرفت is a past tense verb 'took up / set out on' and completes the clause: 'from the Ganges he chose/took the road to China.'
segments.s312-g50: جهان را is the definite object ('the world'); را is the Persian definite object marker. به شمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the sword' (به as instrumental), and در بر گرفت is a compound verbal idea 'took/seized/encompassed' (گرفت, past 'he took'); together: 'he seized the world by the sword.'
segments.s312-g51: نبد is an archaic negative/existential ('نبود' → 'there was not'); روز بیکار = 'an idle day' and تیره شبان = 'dark nights' (تیره = dark/gloomy). The clause asserts that days were not idle and nights not dark — a formula meaning constant activity/vigilance.
segments.s312-g52: طلایه = 'the vanguard/forefront'. به‌روز here functions adverbially 'by day / in daytime'. و 'and' links the halves. به شب = 'by night' or 'at night' (به as an adverbial marker) and پاسبان = 'the guard/watch'. The construction contrasts daytime vanguard with nighttime guard.
segments.s312-g53: بدین گونه is a fixed phrase 'in this manner / thus' (ب as prefix + دین/part of the demonstrative + گونه 'manner'). تا is a directional/extent marker 'to / as far as' and شارستان پدر = 'his father's town' (شارستان = city/town, پدر = father). The whole means 'in this way up to his father's town.'
segments.s312-g54: همی is an adverb indicating continued or habitual action ('still/kept on'); رفت is the past-tense verb 'he went'. گریان is a participial/adjectival complement 'weeping', and پر کینه سر is a prepositional-like phrase 'with (his) head full of hatred' where پر = 'full (of)', کینه = 'hatred/grudge' and سر = 'head' used idiomatically for inner thought/mind. Together: 'he went on, weeping and with his head full of hatred.'
segments.s312-g55: همی marks continuous or iterative action ('he kept on'); گرد باغ سیاوش = 'around Siyavash's garden' (گرد = around/about; باغ سیاوش = Siyavash's garden). بگشت is a past verb 'he went/returned/turned' (here 'wandered/turned about') completing the sense 'he went about the garden.'
segments.s312-g56: به جایی = 'to a place' (به = to/into, جایی = place). که introduces a relative clause 'where/that', and بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put' (here 'had placed'). خونریز modifies تشت as an adjective 'blood-staining / bloody' and تشت = 'a large basin'. The whole clause: 'to the place where he had placed the bloody basin.'
segments.s312-g137: جهانی is used substantively for 'the world' (an adjective used as a noun). به بخت تو is a prepositional/causal phrase ('by/through your fortune'), and آباد گشت is the predicate: adjective آباد + past of گَشتن (گشت) meaning 'became' (i.e. 'became prosperous'). Word order is poetic but straightforward: [subject] [cause phrase] [predicate].
segments.s312-g138: دل دوستداران تو is a genitive/possessive grouping 'the hearts of your admirers' (دوستداران modified by the following pronoun تو). شاد گشت is again adjective + گشت ('became glad'). The order is noun phrase → predicate, typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s312-g139: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایوان ما is the subject 'our portico'. در خور شاه is an older/idiomatic phrase meaning 'befitting/worthy of the king' (در + خور as a fixed collocation). نیست is the negative copula 'is not'. The clause therefore reads 'If our portico is not worthy of the king...'.
segments.s312-g140: گمانم is 'my belief / I suppose' (گمان with first‑person clitic), and که introduces the subordinate clause. هم is an emphatic/also particle ('even' / 'indeed'). بدتر از راه نیست is a comparative negative: 'there is not a worse way/path' — literally 'is not worse than the road', meaning 'I think nothing is worse.'
segments.s312-g141: به کاخ اندر آمد is a prepositional phrase + verb meaning 'entered into the palace' (به ... اندر آمد = 'came into the palace'). سرافراز شاه follows as the subject/epithet 'the glorious / high‑honored king' — poetic inversion places the epithet and subject after the verb.
segments.s312-g142: نشست is the past tense 'sat (down)'. از بر is an archaic locative/relational prepositional phrase meaning 'before / in the presence of'. نامور پیشگاه is the noun phrase 'renowned court/presence' (نامور modifying پیشگاه). Together: 'he sat before the renowned court.'
segments.s312-g143: ز (poetic form of از) introduces the source/material: ز دینار چینی = 'from/with Chinese dinars' (چینی modifies دینار). The second ز + بهر نثار states purpose: 'for the sake of/for offering'. This prepositional cluster sets up the object and purpose before the verb.
segments.s312-g144: بیاورد is the past tense 'brought/produced'. In the poetic word order the agent follows the verb: فغفور چین = 'the faghfur (emperor) of China' as the source/agent, and صد هزار is the numeral object 'a hundred thousand' (understood as dinars from the previous phrase).
segments.s312-g145: همی بود is a habitual/past continuous construction: همی (habitual marker) + بود (past of بودن) = ‘was/stood (habitually or continued). بر پیش او is a locative phrase meaning ‘before/in the presence of him’ (بر as a locative preposition + پیش ‘in the presence of’ + او ‘him’). بر به پای is another locative phrase stacked after it, literally ‘upon/to the foot (of)’, functioning as ‘at the feet (of).’ Word order is poetic: verb phrase appears before the pronoun/agent phrase.
segments.s312-g146: ابا here functions as an archaic/poetic form equivalent to با ‘with’ (a prosthetic alef appears in classical epic diction) and introduces the accompanying group. مرزبانان is the plural noun ‘marzbāns/frontier-guards’; فرخنده رای is a descriptive phrase ‘auspicious/fortunate counsel’ that qualifies the group. Notice the compact epic word order: modifier(s) and descriptive noun cluster follow the head without explicit ezāfeation.
segments.s312-g147: به چین اندرون بود places the locative/goal (به چین اندرون ‘inside/within China’) before the verb; بود is past ‘was’ and خسرو (the subject) follows the verb — a common poetic inversion. سه ماه is a duration phrase (‘three months’) modifying بود. The order emphasizes location and duration before naming the ruler.
segments.s312-g148: ابا (again) functions as a poetic/prepositional element equivalent to با ‘with’ introducing accompaniment. نامداران ایران سپاه is a compact noun phrase: نامداران ‘renowned ones’ + ایران ‘of Iran’ + سپاه ‘the host/army’ — i.e. ‘the army of Iran made up of renowned men.’ The word order places the descriptive noun before the head noun in close epic style.
segments.s312-g149: پرستنده فغفور is a noun–noun apposition: پرستنده (‘worshipper/devotee’) describes فغفور (‘the Byzantine emperor’). هر بامداد is an adverbial time phrase ‘each/every morning’ placed after the noun phrase; in epic Persian temporal adverbials commonly follow the subject phrase.
segments.s312-g150: همی (habitual marker) + نو به نو is a fixed idiomatic sequence meaning ‘anew/one after another’ (literally ‘new to new’). شاه را uses را as the definite object marker marking شاه as the recipient/target. هدیه داد is the verb phrase ‘gave/presented’ (هدیه ‘gift’ + داد ‘gave’); the verb completes the action introduced earlier.
segments.s312-g151: چهارم is an ordinal temporal adverb (‘on the fourth’). ز چین is the ablative/‘from China’. شاه ایران is the subject ‘the Shah of Iran’ (note the noun + proper name sequence). براند is a poetic past form from راندن used here intransitively to indicate departure or movement away (‘left/departed’), so the clause reads ‘on the fourth the Shah of Iran left China.’
segments.s312-g152: به مکران شد is a short verbal phrase meaning ‘(he) came/arrived to Makran’ (به + place + شد, with شد used poetically as an intransitive/resultative verb ‘came to / went to’). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’, and رستم آنجا بماند is ‘Rostam remained there’ (بماند present/past-subjunctive form used as past in epic style = ‘remained’).
segments.s312-g161: Literal order: subject 'جهان' + adjective 'روشن' + cause phrase 'از تاج و بخت' + copula 'منست'. Here 'از' marks the cause/agency ('by/through'), and 'منست' is an archaic fused copula (from 'من است') linking the predicate to the speaker — roughly 'is (by) me' or 'is owing to me'. The whole means 'the world is bright by my crown and fortune.'
segments.s312-g162: Phrase structure: 'سر مهتران' = 'the heads of the great/elders' (classical juxtaposition instead of ezāfe), followed by locative 'زیر تخت' ('under the throne') and the same archaic copula 'منست'. The verb is omitted; 'منست' again functions as a linking verb meaning 'are (under) me / lie under my throne.'
segments.s312-g163: Verb-first classical order: 'برند' (they take/they will take) + temporal adverb 'آنگهی' ('then'), followed by a genitive/subject–object cluster 'دست چیز کسان' — here understood as 'the hands (دست) will take the things of/from people' (چیز کسان = people's things).
segments.s312-g164: 'مگر' introduces an exception/conditional ('unless'). 'مگر من نباشم' uses the negative subjunctive/old present of 'بودن' to mean roughly 'unless I am not / unless I am absent.' 'به هر کس رسان' is a jussive/imperative instruction 'let it be delivered / deliver it to every person' with 'به هر کس' marking the beneficiary. The verb 'رسان' functions as an order or hortative 'deliver/let reach.'
segments.s312-g165: Temporal/causal clause 'علف چون نیابند' = 'when/if they do not find (enough) grass' — 'نیابند' is the negative present/subjunctive form of 'یافتن' meaning 'they do not find/obtain.' The main clause 'جنگ آورند' ('they bring/bring about war') uses 'آورند' (3rd plural) to state the resulting action.
segments.s312-g166: Elliptical clause where 'جهان' is the object of the causative phrase 'تنگ آورند' (to make narrow/hard). 'بر' marks the affected party ('for/against the ill‑minded') and 'بداندیش' describes those affected ('the malicious/ill‑minded'). 'آورند' again is 3rd plural present: 'they cause (it).' The idiom means 'they make life hard for the ill‑minded.'
segments.s312-g167: 'ور' = 'if'; 'ایدون' here is adverbial/temporal 'henceforth/in future' (literally 'come then'), and 'که گفتار من نشنوی' is a subordinate clause 'that you do not hear my speech' — 'نشنوی' is 2nd person singular negative of 'شنیدن' used as present/future conditional 'you do not heed/listen.'
segments.s312-g168: 'به خون فراوان' is an adverbial phrase 'into/among much blood' (i.e., amid great bloodshed) with 'به' = 'into/toward'. 'کس اندر شوی' literally 'you enter/are among people' — here idiomatically 'you will fall among (the slain)' or 'you will end up amid many people in blood.' 'شوی' is 2nd singular present/subjunctive used to indicate the result/future 'you will be/you will become.'
segments.s312-g1: وز آن پس is a poetic temporal phrase meaning 'after that / from then on' (here the initial وز is a connective). بیامد is a past tense 3rd-person singular verb 'came'. خرامان is a manner adverb/participle 'striding/proudly' that modifies the verb; دبیر is the subject 'the secretary' following the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s312-g2: بیاورد here expresses the action/purpose 'to bring' (3rd-person form used in narrative: 'he brought / came to bring'). The direct objects follow the verb in a simple series: قرطاس (paper), مشک (musk), عبیر (fragrance/perfume), linked by و 'and'.
segments.s312-g3: نبشتند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they wrote' (classical/poetic form of نوشتند). نامه is the object 'a letter'. که introduces the content clause 'that ...' and here begins the reported content; از کردگار functions as a prepositional phrase 'by/from the Creator' giving the source/agent in the reported statement.
segments.s312-g4: به before دادیم here has the sense of an instrumental/agentive marker ('by/through'), so به دادیم + از کردگار reads as 'we were given by the Creator' (classical narrative uses simple past 'دادیم' meaning 'we received/ were given'). خشنود از روزگار is an adjectival phrase 'content/pleased with (our) fate/fortune' that completes the reported statement.
segments.s312-g5: که introduces the clause 'that ...'. فرزند ما is the subject 'our son/child' (possessive pronoun after the noun). گشت is the past 3rd-person singular 'became' and پیروزبخت is a predicate adjective 'fortunate/auspicious' following the verb.
segments.s312-g6: سزای is the noun 'the due/deserved reward' (with the genitive/ezāfe understood linking to مهی), مهی is a qualifier 'moon‑like / luminous' modifying سزای. وز here is an archaic/poetic connective (a contraction like و ز = 'and/from' used to link phrases), and در تاج و تخت is a locative phrase 'on/in the crown and throne' that indicates where that honor or due is placed.
segments.s312-g7: بدی را is the definite object 'the evil' (را marks definiteness). که begins a relative clause 'which/that', گیتی is 'the world', همی is an iterative/adverbial 'always/still' and ننگ داشت is a predicate 'held it shameful' (ننگ 'shame', داشت past 'had/held'), so the clause means 'which the world always considered a disgrace.'
segments.s312-g8: جهان را is the definite object 'the world' (here the thing being affected). پر از غارت و جنگ is a predicative phrase 'full of plunder and war' (پر = 'full (of)'), با از/از marks the material 'of' (از غارت و جنگ). داشت is the past transitive 'had / made to be', so the whole clause says that it 'filled the world with plunder and war.'
segments.s312-g25: This is an optative/vocative clause addressed to or invoking the Creator. بداد appears as an archaic/poetic epithet here (not the everyday noun 'oppression'); جهان‌آفرین is the title 'Creator of the world', and شاد باش is an imperative/optative ‘be glad’ (شاد = happy; باش = imperative/optative of بودن). The order places the epithet + title first, then the wish/command.
segments.s312-g26: جهان را is the marked object/topic ('for the world' or 'to the world'); یکی modifies بنیاد as 'one' or 'single'; تازه بنیاد is a compound 'newly founded' or 'freshly established'; and the final باش is the imperative/optative 'be' that issues the wish: 'be (a) single, newly-founded (thing) for the world.' Word order places the object/topic before the predicate which ends in the verb.
segments.s312-g27: مگر here functions as 'unless' (introducing a negative condition). باز = 'again'. بینم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of دیدن used here in the sense 'I may see / I see', so مَگر باز بینم = 'unless I see you again'. تو را is the object-marked 'you', and شادمان is the predicative adjective 'joyful'.
segments.s312-g28: This clause has the pattern 'پر از X گردد Y' = 'Y becomes full of X'. پر از = 'full of', درد = emotional sorrow/pain, گردد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive/optative of شدن used here to mean 'becomes' or 'will become', and دل بدگمان (heart of the distrustful) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s312-g29: وز این پس is an archaic fused expression meaning 'and/from this time on' (وز here is a classical contraction of و + ز = 'and from'); این پس = 'from now on/after this'. جز = 'except', از پیش = 'from the presence/of before', and یزدان پاک = 'the pure God' (یزدان = God, پاک = pure). The phrase sets an exclusive limitation that follows into the next clause.
segments.s312-g30: نباشم is a negative first-person form ('I shall not be / I will not be') here meaning 'I will not have / I will be nothing except...'; کز = 'from whom / because from whom' (literary contraction of که از), اویست is an archaic fused phrase meaning roughly 'it is from Him' or 'from Him is' (here treated as a compound verbal phrase rather than a proper-name), and امید و باک = 'hope and fear'. The clause explains the reason: 'for from Him come hope and fear.'
segments.s312-g31: بدان is the imperative 'know!' formed here from the prefix ب- + دان (دان = 'to know'), giving a direct command 'know this'. تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that' and is followed by the subjunctive باشی ('may you be' / 'you be') with the predicate پیروز ('victorious') and the coordinate شاد ('happy'). The clause expresses purpose/result.
segments.s312-g32: This is a pair of optative wishes introduced by باد as an optative particle ('may it be'). سرت سبز باد = 'may your head be green' (a conventional blessing meaning 'may you prosper'). The second wish uses the possessive suffix ت attached to دل (دلت = 'your heart') and the pattern 'پر ز X' = 'full of X' where X is داد ('justice'); so 'may your heart be full of justice.' Word order puts the optative particle after the adjective (سبز باد) as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s312-g17: پی + او forms an adverbial: پی (in pursuit of) + the pronoun او ('him'). ممان is a verb meaning 'withhold/prevent' (first-person sense: 'I will not let / I will prevent'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'), and نهد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive of نهاد 'to put/place' (here 'to set/put [a foot]'). بر زمین is the prepositional phrase 'on the earth/land' that completes نهد. The order is typical poetic Persian: adverbial (in pursuit of him) + verb (I prevent) + purpose clause (so that he place on the land).
segments.s312-g18: به marks the destination/limit here ('towards/into'); it introduces a list of place names. The proper nouns توران and مکران follow, linked by و ('and'). دریای چین is a genitive (دریای + چین) meaning 'the Sea of China' (دریای is 'sea' taking the following name).
segments.s312-g19: جهان را puts 'the world' in topical/emphatic position (را here marks the definite/affected noun). مگر functions as 'except/if not' ('were it not that' / 'but for'), and ز + و is the poetic contraction for 'from him' (ز = from, the following ו stands in for او 'him' here). رهایی is the noun 'deliverance/freedom', and بود is the past copula 'was' (here describing the existence/state of deliverance). The clause overall means 'the world would have had deliverance from him (were it not otherwise)'.
segments.s312-g20: This is a compact, paradoxical nominal clause. سر ('head') is the topic; بی + بهایش ('without its value/price') is a descriptive phrase modifying سر. بهایی (here a noun/adjective 'a price / value') plus بود ('was') assert that, despite being described as 'بی‌بها' (without worth), the head nonetheless 'had a price'. Word order (topic first, then comment) is typical of poetic/prosaic Persian inversion.
segments.s312-g21: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). داور دادگر is a compound/title: داور 'judge/arbiter' qualified by دادگر 'just', and یک خدای ('one God') indicates uniqueness — the clause names the hypothetical ideal adjudicator. The noun order is typical: condition marker + noun phrase describing the hypothetical judge.
segments.s312-g22: همی is an archaic/emphatic particle (here reinforcing the conditional idea, 'indeed/would'), and بود is the past/copular form used in a counterfactual/conditional sense ('would be'). خواهد is the modal/future auxiliary 'would/will' (here rendering the hypothetical guidance), ترا is the clitic object 'you' (ت + را) and رهنمای is the noun 'guide' (predicate). Together: 'he would (certainly) be your guide.'
segments.s312-g23: که introduces a relative/purpose clause ('that'/'so that'). گیتی is the subject 'the world'. بشویی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of شستن 'to wash/cleanse' ('you may wash/cleanse' or 'you should cleanse') expressing purpose or exhortation. ز رنج means 'from/of suffering'; the following ب + دان appears as the fused form (بدان) in the verse and functions to point to 'that' (literally 'to/for that'); the separate lemmata here are the prefix/preposition and what appears in the data as دان, but in meaning the unit denotes 'that'/'of that' in context. The clause therefore says 'so that you cleanse the world of that suffering.'
segments.s312-g24: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of', introducing the source of the suffering. گفتار و کردار are a paired nominal phrase 'speech and action' (words and deeds), and نابخردان is the plural adjective/noun 'the foolish' that governs the whole phrase (genitive/agent-of-harm reading: the words and deeds of fools).

sentences.s312-s14: Unless I see you again joyful, the distrustful heart will be filled with sorrow.
sentences.s312-s15: From now on I will have none except (be) in the presence of the pure God, for hope and fear come from Him.
sentences.s312-s16: Know this, so that you may be victorious and joyful; may your head be green (flourish) and your heart be full of justice.
sentences.s312-s9: I will pursue him and not allow him to set foot on the lands of Turan and Makran and the Sea of China.
sentences.s312-s10: The world would have been rid of him, were it not that his head — though without value — nevertheless had a price.
sentences.s312-s11: If there were one just judge (one true God), he would indeed be your guide.
sentences.s312-s12: that you might cleanse the world of that suffering, from the words and deeds of the foolish.
